Backreferenced (on pages): [ 22 , 24 , 24 ] low at 28%, yet importantly, as we follow both the compliers and non-compliers, we are able to estimate the treatment effect on the treated (TOT), and the results are nonetheless encouraging. Table  OA-22  Panel A shows the treatment effect on the treated of receiving common financial exposure on values three months after divestment. While there is no effect on corruption perceptions, as one might expect, nonetheless, the treatment effect of stock exposure on the treated shows a durable effect on support for democracy by 0.8 𝜎 (p-value of an increase 0.04), trust in other citizens by 0.43 𝜎 (p-value of an increase 0.16), trust in politicians by 0.59 𝜎 (p-value of an increase 0.04). Those exposed a week before the elections had no chance to begin trading, and the treatment does not effect their voting decisions. However, the effect on those treated a month before the elections is to raise voter turnout by 12.6pp, though this not precisely estimated. 41 We see these results as promising but naturally suggestive at best. More (and more highly powered) research clearly needs to be done, yet these findings provide some hope that treatments that can be designed to incorporate  positive  social spillovers, including shared financial exposures, may be able to counteract some of the negative spillovers of apex corruption on democratic values and societal trust. 41  Panel B reports the intent to treat results, which show consistent patterns, but not surprisingly are weaker given the relatively small shares that actually took up the treatment. Nonetheless, even the intent to treat raises the democracy index by 0.116 𝜎 (p-value of an increase 0.099).

sections.section_name: Do the Effects Accumulate? An Experiment with Two Brothers
paragraphs: While planning fieldwork for the follow-up survey, a second brother of the incumbent president was caught taking bribes on film. This allowed us to create an additional 3-minute corruption video we refer to as the Two Brothers Corruption treatment (henceforth 𝐶𝐼 2 ). This video features sequential footage of bribes being exchanged not just by one but by two siblings of the president. 42 Therefore, after measuring the persistence of the effects from the previous videos, we randomized half of the follow-up (Oaxaca city) sample to receive the Two Brothers corruption video, stratifying by the treatment arms of the first experiment. 43 We are able to measure two different types of accumulation. First, by comparing the half of the control group against the half that got the new  𝐶𝐼 2 video, we can estimate the effect of the two-brother video. We can then benchmark the size of this effect against that of the one -brother 𝐶𝐼 video we estimated above, and ask whether a two-in-one treatment showing evidence for corrupt acts involving two brothers has a larger effect than a similar video showing evidence for just one. Further, we can also examine if there is an accumulating two separate doses effect: i.e., whether people who receive two doses of evidence of apex corruption—one in the first round of the experiment and a second in this round—-suffer larger decreases in support for democracy than those who just got one, i.e., we can test if we can reject the hypothesis  𝛽 𝐶𝐼 2 ≥ 𝛽 𝐶 + 𝛽 𝐶𝐼 2 . To do this, we estimate a regression analogous to equation 1 but interacting the arms of the first experiment with the Two Brothers Corruption video 𝐶𝐼 2 . 44 Because comparison groups are half the size as before, this experiment has less power and the findings are more tentative (see our follow-up trial registration, AEARCTR-0008170 ). Nonetheless, the effects are still informative. As Figure 3 reveals, the two-in-one treatment increases corruption perceptions by 0 . 28 𝜎 and lead to corresponding declines in the democracy index of  − 0 . 23 𝜎 . This decline in immediate expressed support for democracy is about 40 percent larger than the  − 0 . 159 𝜎 we previously found for the one-brother treatment (see Figure 2(b) , p-value of a larger effect: 0.19). The effect of two doses is even more marked. As Figure 3 shows, the effect of the two dose treatment ( 𝛽 𝐶 +  𝛽 𝐶𝐼 2 ) is -0.39 𝜎 . This magnitude is 65% larger than the effect of just the two-in-one video ( 𝐶𝐼 2 ) and 137% larger than the original corruption incumbent ( 𝐶𝐼 ) videos. Further, we can reject |  𝛽 𝐶 + 𝛽 𝐶𝐼 2 |≤|  𝛽 𝐶𝐼 2 | , with p-value=0.03. Taken together, these results suggest apex corruption scandals can have persistent effects in undermin- ing support for democracy. Furthermore, these effects can even accumulate, re-triggering latent attitudes as repeated episodes come to light.

sections.section_name: The Effect of Apex Corruption Revelations across Latin America
paragraphs: Our randomized control trial has helped us establish that apex corruption revelations undermine support for liberal democratic institutions, measured using administrative individual turnout data, natural and incentivized behaviors, and survey measures of values and attitudes. A natural question is whether this key pattern generalizes beyond the Mexican context and whether it can help explain part of the broader macro patterns in democratic backsliding that we observe. To establish this, we can exploit the fact that representative surveys, such as Latinobarometer, have happened to be fielded just before and just after the breaking of apex corruption revelations ‘in the wild’. 45 In particular, we use large-scale survey data combined with the exact timing of apex corruption revelations in 17 Latin American countries from 2008 to 2018 to estimate the impact of apex corruption.

Data on Apex Corruption Revelations. To identify the exact timing of each corruption scandal, we scraped the Twitter news feeds of the 4 major news outlets in each of 17 Latin American countries for the period 2008–2018 (see Table OA-28 for a list) searching for words related to corruption, such as "corrupt," "corruption," "bribery," and "illicit enrichment", among others. We supplemented this with news reports from the LexisNexis Spanish newspapers database using the same words during the same time period. This resulted in 142,000+ news-tweets and 10,000+ LexisNexis news reports for all countries and years. Because we want to focus on apex corruption, we define an event to be a ‘apex corruption scandal’ if it satisfies all of the following conditions: (i) The news event had to be covered by at least two news channels in the country on the same day; (ii) those involved in the scandal had to be high-ranked politicians such as presidents, former presidents, ministers, former ministers, opposition leaders, governors, federal congressmen, high ranked federal directors or federal judges; (iii) the news event had to be a scoop , that is, the scandal had to be a new scandal and not just continued reportage on an existing one. We identified 176 scandal outbreaks over the period that satisfied our apex corruption definition. Some examples of these include: the arrest of former president Lula Da Silva for corruption accusations (Brazil), Former president Mauricio Funes accused of money laundering (El Salvador), President Otto Perez’s involvement in import bribes (Guatemala), that the opposition leader Keiko Fujimori was declared under investigation by the DEA for money laundering (Peru), that a federal program was used by the Minister of Agriculture to give millions in subsidies to drug traffickers (Colombia), President Juan Orlando Hernández identified as a co-conspirator in a drug trafficking scandal (Honduras), and that Ricardo Martinelli was found receiving bribes from Odebrecht during his presidential period (Panama). Given our identification strategy, our final sample of revelations is composed of 28 revelations that intersect with the days when Latinobarometer was being fielded (see Table OA-29 for a list). 46 No country had more than one apex corruption scandal within the survey fielding periods. 47 Latinobarometer  ( 2018 ) conducts a series of yearly nationally representative surveys covering most Latin American countries. Interviews are conducted in person in house-to-house fieldwork that takes about 1-3 months to complete (see Table OA-11 ). The questionnaires are standardized, enabling comparison across 45  We build on the approach used by  Durante et al.  ( 2020 ) to measure the effects of soccer victories on pro-national sentiment in Africa. 46  To the best of our knowledge, our list of apex corruption revelations is comprehensive. Regardless, given our short-run time window, the omission of some events would reduce sample size but should not bias the estimated effect of the included revelations. 47  In a companion study ( González-Téllez et al. , 2025 ) we use the full set of 176 apex corruption scandals. We find that countries so exposed are more likely to exhibit violent, but not non-violent protests. This arguably is again consistent with a decline in democratic values.

countries, and consist of core questions and questions that change across years. We use individual-level survey data from waves of the Latinobarometer conducted between 2008 and 2018 in 17 Latin American countries. 48 Because Latinobarometer field work lasts 28 days on average, we focus on the sample of over 27,000 individuals interviewed in the 15 days before and after the scandal. Analogous to our RCT, we use the survey to measure the perceived general importance of corruption 49 , support for democracy 50 and trust in democratic institutions. 51 Building on the literature (e.g. Acemoglu et al. , 2025b ), and to further unpack the mechanism, we add a fourth outcome: ‘Support for Authoritarian Alternatives’. 52 5.1

Our empirical strategy consists of comparing perceptions of corruption, stated preferences for democ- racy, and trust in democratic institutions, for citizens surveyed a few days after an apex corruption scandal occurs versus those surveyed a few days before, both within the same country and survey round. Con- cretely, we estimate:

since the logistics involved in the implementation of the Latinobarometer survey require many months of preparation (selection of the enumeration sites, setting up of the field teams, etc) and does not change with the occurrence of an apex corruption scandal, making it implausible that the survey dates of a particular type of citizen are correlated with the exact corruption scandal dates. The same identification strategy has been successfully used by Durante et al. ( 2020 ). We further confirm the comparability of the persons interviewed before and after the revelations through balance tests (Table OA-24 ), corruption scandal prediction tests (Table OA-23 ), and other tests. 55 Since, unlike the RCT, we cannot guarantee that participants interviewed after an apex corruption scandal actually know about it at the time of their interview, we interpret the results as intent to treat estimates, that are likely to underestimate the treatment effect on those actually treated with an apex corruption revelation. However, because the apex corruption revelations are entirely divorced from the implementation of the surveys, one advantage of the cross-scandal survey approach that is shared with the administrative turnout analysis in the RCT is that it too is not vulnerable to biases due to experimenter demand effects or social desirability concerns.

sections.section_name: Effects of Apex Corruption Across Scandals in Latin America
paragraphs: Table 5 presents the estimated effects of apex corruption scandals in the wild. Column 1 shows that the fraction of people who think corruption is the most important problem increases by 2.9 𝑝𝑝 (or 31% of the mean) in the 15 days after the scandal compared to the 15 days prior. The effect size is equivalent to the difference between Costa Rica, a nation known for its relative good governance, versus Guatemala. Thus, mimicking our RCT, apex corruption scandals do appear to raise the salience of broader corruption. Further, and again consistent with the RCT, Column 2 shows that support for democracy decreases by 0.069 𝜎 just after the apex corruption scandal. Even though this is an intent-to-treat effect, its magnitude is not small. It is comparable to the difference between Chile and Bolivia in our Democratic Index. Given the population size of Latin America, this fraction is equivalent to 11 million citizens. Finally, note that this is the effect of a single scandal. The effect size implies that 4.7 revelations per country would be enough to explain the decrease in satisfaction with democracy (measured by our index) in the last decade if they accumulated linearly, which as our RCT shows, is quite possible. Column 3 in Table  5  estimates the effect on trust in political institutions. Again consistent with the RCT, we find that trust in democratic institutions decreases by 0.062 𝜎 . Further, Table  OA-26  shows that trust in all four institutions (Congress, Federal Government, Political Parties, and Electoral Authority) declines by about 10% of their respective means.

The decrease in the support for democracy may not necessarily generate an increased preference for authoritarianism, as it may lead to a lack of trust in all forms of government. Further, it is possible that despite their disillusionment with democracy, citizens may still prefer democratic institutions to authoritarian alternatives. Yet, apex corruption could make a more authoritarian government—especially anti-corruption authoritarians who promise to end corruption even at the cost of democratic norms—more 55  Please note that this is not a difference-in-differences specification, nor is it a staggered difference-in-difference: i.e. we are not using control units which will eventually become treated in the future to estimate the treatment effect.

Column 4 shows that citizens’ loss of trust in democracy in the aftermath of an apex corruption scandal does indeed coincide with a rise in support for authoritarianism. We estimate an increase of 3.9 𝑝𝑝 in the authoritarianism index, equivalent to 11% of its mean value.

We next examine how the effects evolve in the days following the scandal. To do this, Figure 4 plots the estimated coefficients and 95% confidence intervals for dummies representing 3-day intervals before and after the apex corruption revelation, using individuals interviewed from 1-3 days prior to the scandal as the reference group. These coefficients are derived from a single regression that controls for individual characteristics and includes country × year fixed effects. 56 As Figure 4 reveals scandal is accompanied by an immediate increase in corruption perceptions, a decrease in support for democracy, a decrease in trust in political institutions, and an increase in the preference for an authoritarian alternative. 57 There is no evidence of decay in the size of the effect; in fact, we cannot reject that the effect is constant.

Placebos and Robustness One key potential concern with this approach is that citizens interviewed right after an apex corruption scandal might simply be different. We test whether this is the case using observable characteristics. Table  OA-24  in the appendix estimates equation  2 , using predetermined demo- graphics as dependent variables. We fail to reject that citizens interviewed before versus after the election have equal characteristics on average. 58 A second concern may be that corruption revelations could be strategically released on certain specific dates, for example, on Mondays when more people may be paying attention to the news. This would be a problem if, e.g. on those same exact Mondays, Latinobarometer 56  This approach benchmarks the responses of individuals, from the same country and year, interviewed 1-3, 4-6, 7-9, 10-12, and 12-15 days after the scandal against those interviewed 1-3 days before the event. These plots serve to evaluate whether the effect is constant and to ensure that no changes in perception or attitudes occurred prior to the scandal. The number of days around the scandal is necessarily limited by the 1-3 month length of Latinobarometro field work, see  OA-10 . 57  While authoritarian governments may be even more corrupt, recent research ( Acemoglu et al. , 2024 , 2025a ) has found that citizens often misperceive regime attributes. 58  We also tested and failed to reject that the number of refusals to have an interview before is equal.

surveyed a selection of people who were already disenchanted with democracy, which is unlikely. Recall that we are making comparisons within a 30-day window while controlling for month, day-of-month, and day-of-week fixed effects. Nonetheless, we test explicitly whether we can predict the day an apex corruption scandal breaks based upon day-of-week, month, day-of-month, and days to the next election. The coefficient estimates on all these variables are not significant, and in-sample accuracy rates are all below 1% (see Appendix Table OA-23 and Figure OA-12 ). In short, the exact day of the revelation of corruption scandals seems orthogonal to these variables. Another potential alternative explanation of the cross-scandal results is that apex corruption revelations generate general negative affect, i.e. bad news causes people to exhibit pessimism across the board. To assess this mechanism, we can examine if responses change on a range of non-political satisfaction indicators. 59 Table  OA-27  shows that we fail to find an effect of apex corruption revelations on any of these variables.

In summary, these cross-scandal comparisons complement the RCT by showing that the effects of apex 59  In particular, we use the question ”In general, would you say that you are very satisfied, rather satisfied, not very satisfied or not at all satisfied with the working of the economic system in the country?” to generate an indicator that takes the value of one if the respondent answered very satisfied or rather satisfied and zero otherwise. A second question is more concrete and asks  ”How would you describe the country’s present economic situation? Would you say it is... very good (=5),..., very bad (=1)?" . A third variable is more ideological and asks for agreement or disagreement with the following statement: “The market economy is the only system with which the country can become a developed country” .

corruption are not limited to the specificities of the Mexican context or the specifics of how the experiment was conducted. Instead the effects of apex corruption in undermining support for liberal democracy and to increase the desire for an authoritarian alternative appear to generalize.

Scandals implicating those at the very highest levels of government in democratic states in corrupt activities have been all-too-common, even while support for democratic institutions declines. In this paper, combining evidence both from ’apex corruption in the wild’ and a randomized control trial, we show that such apex corruption erodes democratic values and support for democratic institutions, reduces citizens’ vote turnout and their engagement in costly activities in support of democratic organizations. Apex corruption also appears to undermine citizens’ internalized norms of honesty and trust in democratic institutions and in one another. Furthermore, we find that the effects on democratic values persist over time and accumulate in the face of further evidence of corruption. Thus, while it may seem at first that disclosure of apex corruption is positive for democratic institutions, enhancing transparency and accountability, there appears to be a darker side to disclosing apex corruption: an erosion of democratic values. 60 Our paper points to potential paths forward. While our intervention priming common national identity proved not to enhance broad support for democracy, our intervention providing equity in the broader economy through common financial exposures holds some promise. Further, just as corrupt acts by highly-placed individuals can have broad negative impacts, there is also a possibility that evidence of  apex honesty  by high-profile leaders can rebuild societal trust and support for democracy. Our paper raises several important questions for future research. First, could evidence of apex honesty be effective for reducing corruption and building trust? Second, are the effects of apex corruption similar in authoritarian regimes? Do prominent displays of judicial enforcement and punishment of those implicated in apex corruption scandals serve to restore trust in the system? 61 Finally, which policies could counteract the negative impacts of apex corruption and help boost democratic values? With support for democracy eroding to worrying levels around the world, the need to better understand how to counteract the effects of apex corruption is great indeed.

60  Indeed in our follow-on paper, González-Téllez et al. ( 2025 ), we find that citizens in places exposed to apex corruption are more likely to resort to violence rather than relying on the democratic process to settle disagreements. 61  Sample size limitations and the fact that many scandals go unpunished and take many years in court limited such a test in this paper.
